section.title: Offerings, Prayer, and David's Death
section.sectionRationale: The book's final movement gathers every thread: David's own vast gift for the temple, the whole assembly's willing offerings, David's magnificent closing prayer of praise and confession, Solomon's second, public coronation, and a brief, dignified summary of David's long reign and death. It is the theological and narrative capstone of 1 Chronicles, ending the story of David not with his sins but with worship, generosity, and a kingdom peacefully handed on.
section.thesis: David caps his reign not with conquest but with worship, giving lavishly for the temple, calling all Israel to give as willingly, and praying a prayer that confesses the whole truth of the book, that greatness, wealth, and even the gifts God's people bring back to Him all come from His own hand first, so that Solomon's peaceful coronation and David's honored death close 1 Chronicles on a note of grateful stewardship that becomes a model for the exiles, and for every reader since, of what it means to give back to God what was always His.
section.frameNote: Four movements: David's personal gift and public call for freewill offerings; the assembly's joyful, generous response; David's prayer of blessing before all Israel; and Solomon's second coronation with the closing summary of David's reign and death.

units.label: David's gift and the call to give
units.note: who is willing to consecrate himself today

verses.text: Then King David said to the whole assembly, "My son Solomon, the one whom God has chosen, is young and inexperienced. The task is great because this palace is not for man, but for the LORD God.

Now with all my ability I have made provision for the house of my God, gold for the gold articles, silver for the silver, bronze for the bronze, iron for the iron, and wood for the wood, as well as onyx for the settings, turquoise, stones of various colors, all kinds of precious stones, and slabs of marble, all in abundance.
Moreover, because of my delight in the house of my God, I now give for it my personal treasures of gold and silver, over and above all that I have provided for this holy temple:
three thousand talents of gold (the gold of Ophir) and seven thousand talents of refined silver, to overlay the walls of the buildings,
for the gold work and the silver work, and for all the work to be done by the craftsmen. Now who is willing to consecrate himself to the LORD today?"

units.label: The assembly gives willingly
units.note: freely and wholeheartedly, and the people rejoiced

verses.text: Then the leaders of the families, the officers of the tribes of Israel, the commanders of thousands and of hundreds, and the officials in charge of the king's work gave willingly.
Toward the service of God's house they gave 5,000 talents and 10,000 darics of gold, 10,000 talents of silver, 18,000 talents of bronze, and 100,000 talents of iron.
Whoever had precious stones gave them to the treasury of the house of the LORD, under the care of Jehiel the Gershonite.
And the people rejoiced at the willing response of their leaders, for they had given to the LORD {{nedavah|freely}} and wholeheartedly. And King David also rejoiced greatly.

units.label: David's prayer of blessing
units.note: Yours, O LORD, is the greatness

verses.text: Then David blessed the LORD in the sight of all the assembly and said: "May You be blessed, O LORD, God of our father Israel, from everlasting to everlasting.

Yours, O LORD, is the {{gedullah|greatness}} and the power and the glory and the splendor and the majesty, for everything in heaven and on earth belongs to You. Yours, O LORD, is the kingdom, and You are exalted as head over all.
Both riches and honor come from You, and You are the ruler over all. In Your hands are power and might to exalt and give strength to all.
Now therefore, our God, we give You thanks, and we praise Your glorious name.
But who am I, and who are my people, that we should be able to give as generously as this? For everything comes from You, and from Your own hand we have given to You.
For we are foreigners and strangers in Your presence, as were all our forefathers. Our days on earth are like a shadow, without hope.
O LORD our God, from Your hand comes all this abundance that we have provided to build You a house for Your holy Name, and all of it belongs to You.

I know, my God, that You test the heart and delight in uprightness. All these things I have given willingly and with an upright heart, and now I have seen Your people who are present here giving joyfully and willingly to You.
O LORD, God of our fathers Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, keep this desire forever in the intentions of the hearts of Your people, and direct their hearts toward You.
And give my son Solomon a whole heart to keep and carry out all Your commandments, decrees, and statutes, and to build Your palace for which I have made provision."

units.label: Solomon crowned, David's reign summarized
units.note: a second time, before the LORD

verses.text: Then David said to the whole assembly, "Bless the LORD your God." So the whole assembly blessed the LORD, the God of their fathers. They bowed down and paid homage to the LORD and to the king.

The next day they offered sacrifices and presented burnt offerings to the LORD: a thousand bulls, a thousand rams, and a thousand lambs, along with their drink offerings, and other sacrifices in abundance for all Israel.

That day they ate and drank with great joy in the presence of the LORD. Then, for a second time, they designated David's son Solomon as king, anointing him before the LORD as ruler, and Zadok as the priest.
So Solomon sat on the throne of the LORD as king in place of his father David. He prospered, and all Israel obeyed him.
All the officials and mighty men, as well as all of King David's sons, pledged their allegiance to King Solomon.
The LORD highly exalted Solomon in the sight of all Israel and bestowed on him royal majesty such as had not been bestowed on any king in Israel before him.
David son of Jesse was king over all Israel.
The length of David's reign over Israel was forty years, seven years in Hebron and thirty-three years in Jerusalem.
He died at a ripe old age, full of years, riches, and honor, and his son Solomon reigned in his place.
Now the acts of King David, from first to last, are indeed written in the Chronicles of Samuel the Seer, the Chronicles of Nathan the Prophet, and the Chronicles of Gad the Seer,
together with all the details of his reign, his might, and the circumstances that came upon him and Israel and all the kingdoms of the lands.

nedavah.gloss: Freewill offering, a voluntary gift given willingly, not under compulsion or obligation. Twice this chapter stresses the assembly gave to the LORD nedavah, freely, and with a whole heart, verse 9, echoing David's own earlier gift given purely 'because of my delight in the house of my God,' verse 3, rather than from duty alone.

gedullah.gloss: Greatness, majesty. The opening word of David's prayer's great ascription, 'Yours, O LORD, is the gedullah,' verse 11, piled up alongside power, glory, splendor, and majesty, to declare that every quality of true greatness belongs first and finally to God, not to David's kingdom, however magnificent it appeared at that moment.

basic.context: In the final chapter of 1 Chronicles, David, now near the end of his life, addresses the whole assembly one last time. He explains that Solomon, though young and inexperienced, faces an enormous task, and reveals the staggering personal gift he has already given for the temple beyond all his earlier public provision, then asks who else is willing to give freely to the LORD. The leaders of Israel respond with overwhelming generosity, and the whole people rejoice at their willing hearts. David then prays one of the most treasured prayers in Scripture, blessing God as the source of all greatness, riches, and power, confessing that even the assembly's generous gifts were never truly theirs to give, since everything comes from God's hand first. He prays that this generous spirit would remain in Israel's hearts forever and specifically asks God to give Solomon a whole heart to keep His commandments. The next day, sacrifices are offered in abundance, and Solomon is anointed king a second time, this time publicly and joyfully before the whole nation, with Zadok anointed as priest alongside him. The book closes with a brief, dignified summary: David reigned forty years, died at a ripe old age full of years, riches, and honor, and his deeds were recorded in other now-lost writings.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a specific, climactic moment of transition and worship at the end of David's reign; it is not a fundraising manual, though its theology of giving remains fully applicable. The chapter's central theological claim, that all giving to God is simply returning what already belongs to Him, verse 14, is meant to be received and lived out by every generation of God's people, not confined to David's own temple-building moment.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is bookended by two rich words: nedavah, the freewill offering given not from compulsion but delight, describing both David's personal gift, verse 3, and the assembly's response, verse 9; and gedullah, greatness, the first word of David's prayer's great ascription, verse 11, declaring that every kind of true greatness, power, glory, splendor, and majesty, belongs to God alone, never ultimately to any human king, however magnificent his reign.
basic.typology: David's prayer confessing that all the assembly's wealth was only ever God's own gift returned to Him anticipates the New Testament's teaching that every good and perfect gift comes down from the Father of lights, James 1:17, and that believers are simply stewards, not owners, of what God entrusts to them, 1 Corinthians 4:7. Solomon's peaceful, joyful, second coronation before all Israel foreshadows the far greater installation of Christ, the true Son of David, whom God has highly exalted and given the name above every name, Philippians 2:9.
basic.christ: David's prayer, 'Yours, O LORD, is the kingdom, and You are exalted as head over all,' verse 11, anticipates the full revelation of God's kingdom in Christ, to whom every knee will bow, Philippians 2:10-11. Solomon's coronation, celebrated with sacrifice and universal allegiance, prefigures the greater enthronement of Jesus, the final Son of David, whose kingdom, unlike Solomon's, will never end or divide, Luke 1:32-33. And David's own life, summarized here in a single verse of quiet dignity after such a turbulent, sin-marked reign, points to a King who needed no such summary of failure alongside triumph, Jesus, whose entire life pleased His Father perfectly, from first to last.
basic.god: God is the source of all greatness, power, glory, splendor, and majesty, the ruler over all, in whose hands alone lie the power to exalt and give strength, verses 11-12. He is generous beyond measure, giving David and Israel everything they have, so that even their most extravagant gifts back to Him are, as David confesses, simply 'from Your own hand we have given to You,' verse 14. And He is the God who tests the heart and delights in uprightness, verse 17, caring far more about the willingness and wholeness of the giver's heart than the size of the gift itself.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Chronicles 28:9 records David's earlier charge to Solomon to serve God with a whole heart, the same request David now prays God would grant, verse 19. 2 Corinthians 9:7 teaches that God loves a cheerful giver, echoing the joyful, willing giving celebrated in this chapter. James 1:17 declares every good gift comes down from the Father of lights. Philippians 2:9-11 pictures the exaltation of Christ, the true fulfillment of every earthly coronation, including Solomon's here.
basic.oneStory: 1 Chronicles closes not with David's failures but with worship: lavish giving, a prayer confessing that all things come from God, and a peaceful, joyful coronation. This is the theological capstone of the whole book's argument, that Israel's true hope rests not in David's own greatness but in the LORD's, and that hope reaches forward through Solomon's temple and throne to Christ, David's greater Son, the true and everlasting King to whom all worship, giving, and greatness ultimately belong.

context.literary: This final chapter draws together every major thread of 1 Chronicles, worship, the temple, David's kingship, and the whole assembly's participation, into a single, climactic scene of giving, prayer, coronation, and death. Structurally it moves from private gift, to public offering, to prayer, to public sacrifice and coronation, to a formal closing summary, the standard pattern by which Chronicles closes its accounts of each king's reign.
context.historical: The staggering sums recorded, three thousand talents of gold from David alone, plus the assembly's further five thousand talents and ten thousand darics of gold, reflect the accumulated wealth of decades of conquest and tribute, all now directed toward a single sacred purpose rather than personal luxury or further war. Solomon's coronation 'for a second time,' verse 22, likely refers back to an earlier private or provisional anointing, perhaps 1 Kings 1, now confirmed and celebrated publicly before the whole assembly to remove any doubt about the succession, following Absalom's and Adonijah's earlier attempted usurpations.
context.canonical: David's prayer here, especially 'yours, O LORD, is the greatness, and the power, and the glory,' verse 11, echoes so closely in the church's memory that it shaped the doxology later added to the Lord's Prayer, 'for Thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever.' The chapter's insistence that all giving is simply returning God's own gifts anchors a theology of stewardship traced through the whole canon, from the tithe and firstfruits of the Torah to the New Testament's call to give cheerfully because everything is already God's, 2 Corinthians 8:1-5 and 9:6-15.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To close 1 Chronicles with David's own lavish, willing gift for the temple, the assembly's matching generosity, David's prayer confessing that all such giving simply returns what God had first given, and Solomon's peaceful, public coronation, so that the whole book ends not on David's failures but on worship, stewardship, and hope in the throne God had promised would endure forever.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical act of giving and a specific coronation; it does not prescribe that churches must collect precise quantities of gold, silver, and bronze. What is timelessly prescriptive is the theology behind the giving, that all resources belong to God first, that giving is meant to be free and wholehearted rather than compelled, and that such generosity flows naturally from truly knowing who God is.

debates.question: Why does David's prayer include the stark, seemingly bleak line, 'we are foreigners and strangers in Your presence... our days on earth are like a shadow, without hope,' verse 15?

views.label: A humbling confession of human transience before God's eternity
views.case: In the very moment of Israel's greatest recorded generosity and triumph, David deliberately punctures any pride by confessing that human life itself is fleeting and insubstantial compared to the everlasting LORD, verse 10, a needed humility lest such wealth and success breed self-reliance rather than continued dependence on God.

views.label: An echo of the patriarchs' own self-understanding as sojourners
views.case: The phrase 'foreigners and strangers... as were all our forefathers' deliberately recalls how Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob described themselves while living in the promised land without yet possessing it, Genesis 23:4 and 47:9, tying David's generation to that same pilgrim identity even at the height of national success and settled kingship.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same lesson: even Israel's most secure, wealthy, and settled moment under David is still lived out with the humility of pilgrims who possess nothing ultimately their own, a posture the New Testament commends fully, Hebrews 11:13.

debates.question: Why was Solomon anointed king 'for a second time,' verse 22?

views.label: Confirming an earlier private or emergency anointing
views.case: 1 Kings 1:32-40 records Solomon anointed hastily by Zadok and Nathan while David was still alive, specifically to preempt Adonijah's attempted coup; this chapter's public, joyful coronation before the whole assembly, complete with sacrifice, functions as the fuller, national confirmation and celebration of that earlier, more urgent anointing.

views.label: A deliberate parallel to David's own two anointings
views.case: David himself was anointed privately by Samuel long before his public acknowledgment as king over all Israel, 1 Samuel 16:13 and 2 Samuel 5:3; the Chronicler may be deliberately patterning Solomon's succession after David's own two-stage installation, underscoring continuity and legitimacy in the transfer of the throne.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same central point: Solomon's kingship is publicly, joyfully, and unmistakably confirmed before the entire nation, removing any lingering doubt about the succession God had promised.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's theology of giving turns on nedavah, the freewill offering given not from compulsion but from delight, used of both David's own extraordinary personal gift, verse 3, and the whole assembly's matching response, verse 9, the same joyful, willing spirit the New Testament later commends in 2 Corinthians 9:7. David's prayer opens with gedullah, greatness, piled together with power, glory, splendor, and majesty, verse 11, insisting that every quality of true magnificence, however dazzling David's own kingdom appeared at that moment, belongs finally and only to the LORD, from whose hand, David confesses, even Israel's most generous gifts had first come, verse 14.

typology.type: David's confession that all giving simply returns God's own gifts
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's teaching that every good and perfect gift comes from the Father, and that believers are stewards, not owners, James 1:17 and 1 Corinthians 4:7.

typology.type: Solomon's joyful, public, second coronation before all Israel
typology.fulfillment: The exaltation of Christ, the true Son of David, given the name above every name so that every knee will bow, Philippians 2:9-11.

typology.type: David's prayer, 'Yours, O LORD, is the kingdom... and You are exalted as head over all'
typology.fulfillment: The full and final revelation of God's kingdom in Christ, whose reign will never end, Luke 1:32-33 and Revelation 11:15.

study.christ: David's great prayer, 'Yours, O LORD, is the kingdom, and You are exalted as head over all,' verse 11, finds its fullest answer in Jesus, to whom God has given a name above every name, so that at His name every knee will bow and every tongue confess that He is Lord, Philippians 2:9-11. Solomon's joyful, public coronation, celebrated with abundant sacrifice and the whole nation's allegiance, prefigures the far greater installation of Christ, the true and final Son of David, whose kingdom, unlike Solomon's soon-divided one, will never end, Luke 1:32-33. And where 1 Chronicles must close David's story with a single honest sentence of both triumph and the unspoken memory of real failure, verses 26-28, the whole of Scripture points to Jesus, the greater Son of David whose life needed no such qualified summary, and who alone secures forever the everlasting throne this book has been tracing from its opening genealogies.
study.god: God is the source and owner of all greatness, power, glory, splendor, and majesty, ruling over all, holding in His hands alone the power to exalt and give strength, verses 11-12. He is astonishingly generous, having given David and Israel everything they possess, so that their most lavish gift back to Him is, in David's own honest words, simply 'from Your own hand we have given to You,' verse 14. He tests the heart and delights in uprightness rather than mere outward show, verse 17, and He is faithful across an entire lifetime, bringing David, despite real and serious failures recorded elsewhere in Scripture, to a death described here with the dignity of 'a ripe old age, full of years, riches, and honor,' verse 28.

crossReferences.note: David's earlier charge to Solomon to serve God with a whole heart, the very gift David now prays God would grant his son, verse 19.
Solomon's earlier, more urgent anointing amid Adonijah's attempted coup, likely the 'first time' behind this chapter's 'second time' coronation, verse 22.
Paul's teaching that God loves a cheerful giver and that all generosity flows from God's own indescribable gift, closely paralleling this chapter's theology of giving.
Every good and perfect gift comes down from the Father of lights, the New Testament's clearest echo of David's confession in verse 14.
God's exaltation of Christ above every name, the final and complete fulfillment of David's prayer that the LORD alone is exalted as head over all.

study.oneStory: The book of 1 Chronicles, which began with page after page of genealogy stretching back to Adam, closes here with an old king pouring out his wealth, his people giving freely alongside him, and a prayer confessing that every good thing they possess, and even their generosity itself, was always God's own gift returned to Him. From Adam to David, from the fracture of Eden to the ark restored to worship to a temple now fully planned and funded, this whole story has been building toward a throne that would last forever. David dies in peace, Solomon is crowned amid rejoicing, and the reader is left looking further still, past Solomon's coming glory and eventual failure, to Jesus Christ, David's greater Son, in whom the everlasting kingdom, and the greatness, and the glory, finally and forever belong to the LORD.

sermon.title: David's Prayer at Death
sermon.why: Preached by Timothy Keller at Redeemer Presbyterian Church on November 18, 2007, in the series Real Spirituality, Prayer and Beyond, on 1 Chronicles 29:6-20, this sermon works through David's prayer and the assembly's giving to show that generosity flows from and reveals real spiritual encounter with God, directly expounding this chapter's central movement of offering and prayer.

quiz.q: Beyond all his earlier public provision, what did David personally give for the temple, according to verses 3-4?

options.text: His own personal treasures of gold and silver, including three thousand talents of gold and seven thousand talents of refined silver, given because of his delight in God's house.
options.note: Verses 3-4 record this specific, lavish personal gift, given over and above everything already provided.

options.text: Only his time and organizational effort, since his wealth had already been fully spent on earlier provisions.
options.note: The text specifically describes an additional, enormous personal gift of gold and silver, not merely time or effort.

options.text: A large gift of gold and silver, though the text does not specify any particular amount.
options.note: True that David gave gold and silver from personal delight in God's house, but the text does specify precise amounts, three thousand talents of gold and seven thousand talents of silver, verse 4.

options.text: A modest, symbolic gift meant mainly to inspire others rather than materially fund the project.
options.note: The amounts given, three thousand talents of gold alone, were anything but modest or merely symbolic; this was a substantial material contribution.

quiz.q: How did the assembly of leaders respond to David's call to give, according to verses 6-9?

options.text: They gave willingly and generously, and the people rejoiced at their willing hearts, as did David.
options.note: Verses 6-9 describe abundant, willing giving met with joyful celebration throughout the assembly.

options.text: They gave only reluctantly, feeling pressured by David's example.
options.note: The text specifically stresses the giving was willing and joyful, the opposite of reluctant compulsion.

options.text: They gave generously, though the text does not comment on the people's emotional response.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly describes the people rejoicing and David rejoicing greatly, a clear emotional response the text does comment on.

options.text: Only the wealthiest officials gave, while ordinary people were not invited to contribute.
options.note: The named givers are indeed the leaders, officers, and officials, verse 6, but 'the people' are also explicitly said to rejoice at the response, suggesting the wider assembly's engagement even if verse 6 names their leaders as the specific givers listed.

quiz.q: What central confession does David make about the source of the assembly's generous gifts, in verse 14?

options.text: That everything comes from God, and from His own hand they have simply given back to Him.
options.note: Verse 14 states this precisely: 'everything comes from You, and from Your own hand we have given to You.'

options.text: That the gifts prove Israel's own righteousness and worthiness before God.
options.note: David's prayer does the opposite, humbly asking 'who am I, and who are my people' rather than claiming worthiness.

options.text: That the gifts were mostly the fruit of Israel's successful wars and trade.
options.note: While the wealth did come partly through conquest, David's prayer credits God as the ultimate source, not merely crediting military or economic success.

options.text: That the gifts were generous by human standards, though ultimately God needs nothing from Israel.
options.note: It is true that God is not dependent on human gifts, but David's specific confession in verse 14 is that the gifts themselves originated from God's hand in the first place, not merely a statement of God's self-sufficiency.

quiz.q: What did David specifically ask God to give his son Solomon in his closing prayer, verse 19?

options.text: A whole heart to keep and carry out all God's commandments, decrees, and statutes, and to build the temple.
options.note: Verse 19 records this exact request, echoing David's earlier charge in chapter 28:9.

options.text: Great wealth and military success surpassing his own reign.
options.note: David's specific request concerns Solomon's heart and obedience, not wealth or military conquest.

options.text: Wisdom to rule Israel well, a gift closely associated with Solomon elsewhere in Scripture.
options.note: Wisdom is indeed famously granted to Solomon later, 1 Kings 3, but this specific prayer asks God for a whole heart to keep His commandments, decrees, and statutes, not explicitly for wisdom.

options.text: A long life and many descendants to continue his dynasty.
options.note: The prayer's specific request is for wholehearted obedience and completion of the temple, not length of life or number of descendants.

quiz.q: How does the book of 1 Chronicles summarize David's reign and death, in verses 26-28?

options.text: He reigned forty years over Israel and died at a ripe old age, full of years, riches, and honor, with Solomon succeeding him.
options.note: Verses 26-28 give this concise, dignified summary of David's forty-year reign and honored death.

options.text: He died suddenly and unexpectedly in battle, leaving the succession disputed.
options.note: The text describes a peaceful death at an old age, with the succession already publicly and joyfully secured in this very chapter.

options.text: He reigned forty years, entirely in Jerusalem, after conquering the city as a young man.
options.note: Verse 27 specifically divides his forty years between seven years in Hebron and thirty-three in Jerusalem, not the entirety in Jerusalem alone.

options.text: He reigned honorably, though the text pointedly criticizes his failures in detail at his life's close.
options.note: David's failures are real and recorded elsewhere in Scripture, but this closing summary in Chronicles is notably brief and honoring, not a detailed critique, choosing instead to end on 'a ripe old age, full of years, riches, and honor.'

questions: David gave 'because of my delight in the house of my God,' not from mere duty. Where does your own giving, of money, time, or gifts, come from delight in God rather than obligation?

David's prayer confesses that even Israel's most generous gifts were simply 'from Your own hand we have given to You.' How does seeing everything you own as already God's change the way you think about ownership and generosity?

David prayed that God would keep this generous desire 'forever in the intentions of the hearts of Your people.' What practices help keep your own heart oriented toward willing, joyful generosity rather than letting it fade over time?

David asked God to give Solomon a whole heart to obey, not merely wisdom or success. As you think about someone you love or lead, what would it look like to pray for their wholehearted devotion to God above every other blessing?

1 Chronicles closes David's story briefly and honorably, 'full of years, riches, and honor,' without dwelling on his failures. As you think about finishing your own life or a season of work well, what would it mean to you to be remembered for faithfulness rather than failure?

translationNotes.note: The BSB lists a long series of materials David provided; source em dashes marking this list, 'gold for the gold articles, silver for the silver' and so on, were converted to commas throughout.

The footnote's estimate that three thousand talents is approximately 113 tons or 103 metric tons of gold, and seven thousand talents approximately 264 tons or 239.5 metric tons of silver, gives a sense of the staggering scale of David's personal gift alone.

The footnotes give approximate modern equivalents for the assembly's gifts: 5,000 talents of gold is about 188.5 tons, 10,000 talents of silver about 377 tons, 18,000 talents of bronze about 678.6 tons, and 100,000 talents of iron about 3,770 tons, underscoring the scale of the whole nation's generosity alongside David's own.
The BSB renders the division of David's forty-year reign, seven years in Hebron and thirty-three in Jerusalem, as a single clause; a source em dash was converted to a comma for a clean break.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 29:1-30. Source em dashes throughout, especially in the lists of materials and sums in verses 2, 7, and 27, were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file.
